examples.text: فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِن كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْمًا يَجْعَلُ الْوِلْدَانَ شِيبًا
examples.english: So if you disbelieve, how can you guard yourselves against a Day that will turn children’s hair grey,

senses.gloss: the product of, the result of, occasioned by, engendered by, sprung from

etymology.text:
Related to Hebrew יָלִיד (yalíd).

Diminutive of وَلَد (walad).

body.text: in the sense of the measure
signify the same, (T, L, K,) i. e.,
(the former in the L, and the latter in the Msb:) the former, as well as the latter, masc.: (M, L:) or, accord. to some, the former is applied also to a female: as also ↓
The child that dies in early infancy,
that is prematurely born, is in paradise.
a boy who has arrived at the age when he is fit for service, before he attains to puberty:
one is so called from the time of his birth until
he attains to manhood: the servant of a man in paradise is a
(S, L, K;) or, as some say, one
: (S, A, L, K:) a female slave is called
even if aged: (L:) pl. (of the masc., S, L)

body.text_bw: humo fiY A^amori laA yunaAdaY waliyduhu
body.text_ar: هُمْ فِى اَمْرِ لَا يُنَادَى وَلِيدُهُ

body.text: slave, will not be called out to

]: a proverb, (L,) originally meaning, they are in a case of difficulty or distress, such that the mother forgets her child, and does not call out to him: and afterwards applied to any case of difficulty or distress: (M, L:) or they are in a formidable case, in which children are not called out to, but those advanced in age: (AO, or As, M, L:) and sometimes it means, they are in such a state of abundance and affluence that if a
put forth his hand to take a thing he is not chidden away from it: (M, L:) or it is applied to a case of good and to one of evil, and means, they are so occupied with their case or affair that if a

put forth his hand to the most valuable of things he is not called out to for the purpose of chiding him: (K:) some say, that its original reference is to the running of horses; because a fleet and excellent horse goes without being called out to; and that it is secondarily applied to any case of great moment, and to any case of abundance. (S, L.) ―         -b7-  One also says,
In the land is fresh herbage respecting which the servant-boy,
slave, will not be called out to
]; because it matters not in what part of such land the beasts are; the whole abounding with herbage: and
They brought food respecting which the servant-boy,
slave, would not be called out to
]; meaning, that one would not care what injury he might do to it, nor when he ate of it. (ISk, L.) ―         -b8-  Muzarrid Eth-Thaalebee says,

tabar~aA^otu mano $atomi Alr~ijaAli bitawobapK
                      
                     
                        A_ilaY A@ll~`hi min~iY laA yunaAdaY waliyduhaA
I have become clear of the vice of reviling men, by my turning unto God with repentance respecting which the servant
will not be called out to
]; meaning, respecting which I shall not be questioned. (ISk, L)
